coredata.dc:title: Unraveling the carbon dot bridges in oxidized carbon nanotubes for efficient microwave absorption
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Jie
Wang, Liuying
Wang, Bin
Liu, Gu
Ge, Chaoqun
Wang, Long
Wang, Weichao
Xu, Kejun
Chen, Mengzhou
Wang, Wenhao
Hu, Lingjie
Chen, Haiqing
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanotube (CNT)-based composites hold immense promise in the realm of electromagnetic (EM) wave absorption. Surface functionalization of CNTs plays a pivotal role in augmenting their surface binding energy, thereby bolstering their affinity for composites. However, the incorporation of functional groups may disrupt the electronic configuration of sp2 plus delocalized π-bonds and impede electronic transport within the CNT framework. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) were introduced onto the surface of oxidized CNTs (O-CNTs) using a one-step hydrothermal method to enhance their bonding capability and microwave absorption performance. Analysis showed that the characteristic functional groups on the surfaces of the CDs formed amide bonds between the CNTs, bridging them with π-conjugated structures and repairing the blocked electron transport pathway triggered by carboxyl groups. The bridging accelerated the electron flow between adjacent CNTs and enhanced the electron jumping and migration in the CDs-CNTs system. Additionally, a comprehensive investigation of the heterogeneous charge distribution within the CDs bridges and their tunable band gap demonstrated their significant influence on polarization relaxation and conduction loss. In short, the study presents a novel functionalization theory for CNTs that exhibit remarkable microwave-absorption performance in X-band.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanotubes
Carbon dots
Electron transport
Amide bonds
Microwave-absorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the crude products of N-free-CDs
Synthesis of the crude products of B-CDs and G-CDs
Synthesis of N-free-CDs-CNTs, B-CDs-CNTs and G-CDs-CNTs
The electron-transfer kinetic process of CDs-CNTs composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi (No. 2022JQ-356 ); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2022M723884 ); Special Support Program for High-level Talents of Shaanxi Province (No. 2020-44 ). We also thank The Youth Innovation Team of Shaanxi Universities for the guidance on methodology.

This work was supported by Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi (No.2022JQ-356); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022M723884); Special Support Program for High-level Talents of Shaanxi Province (No. 2020-44). We also thank The Youth Innovation Team of Shaanxi Universities for the guidance on methodology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The EDS elemental mapping of C, O and N, and b) the HR-TEM image of CDs-CNTs. c) The schematic diagram of CDs-CNTs. The Fourier-filtered lattice images along the d) (002) lattice planes and e) (001) lattice planes of the targeted areas. f) The FT-IR spectra, g) the Raman spectra, and h) the EIS spectra of CNTs, O-CNTs and CDs-CNT. High-resolution i) C 1s, j) O 1s, and k) N 1s XPS spectra.

A) emission spectra and b) the pl-decay curves of cds and cds-cnts. c) the possible mechanism of the cds and cds-cnts. differential charge density distributions of d) covalent interactions, e) non-covalent interactions and f) integrals of differential charge densities for y and z axes.

The TEM image, the HR-TEM image, and the histogram of the particle size distribution of a) B-CDs and b) G-CDs. c) Emission spectra and the inset shows the possible mechanism of B-CDs and G-CDs. d) The charge distributions of LUMO and HOMO and e) energy levels of model of B-CDs and G-CDs. f) Electrostatic potential map of CDs and N-free-CDs.

Frequency dependence of real part and imaginary part of a) dielectric constant and b) magnetic permeability of CNTs, O-CNTs, N-free-CDs-CNTs, B-CDs-CNTs and G-CDs-CNTs. The reflection loss 3D-diagrams in various thicknesses of c) CNTs, d) O-CNTs, e) N-free-CDs-CNTs, f) B-CDs-CNTs and g) G-CDs-CNTs. h) Microwave absorption curves of the best performed G-CDs-CNTs samples with optimal thicknesses.
a) The bonding model of CDs-CNTs, and b) the simulated composite diagram of CDs and CNTs from a microscopic perspective. c) Simulation of electron transfer process between CDs and CNTs.
Parameters of the TRPL decay curves of CDs and CDs-CNTs.

head.ce:title: Unraveling the carbon dot bridges in oxidized carbon nanotubes for efficient microwave absorption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhijian Laboratory, Rocket Force University of Engineering, Xi’an 710025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhijian Laboratory, Rocket Force University of Engineering, Xi’an 710025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanotube (CNT)-based composites hold immense promise in the realm of electromagnetic (EM) wave absorption. Surface functionalization of CNTs plays a pivotal role in augmenting their surface binding energy, thereby bolstering their affinity for composites. However, the incorporation of functional groups may disrupt the electronic configuration of sp2 plus delocalized π-bonds and impede electronic transport within the CNT framework. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) were introduced onto the surface of oxidized CNTs (O-CNTs) using a one-step hydrothermal method to enhance their bonding capability and microwave absorption performance. Analysis showed that the characteristic functional groups on the surfaces of the CDs formed amide bonds between the CNTs, bridging them with π-conjugated structures and repairing the blocked electron transport pathway triggered by carboxyl groups. The bridging accelerated the electron flow between adjacent CNTs and enhanced the electron jumping and migration in the CDs-CNTs system. Additionally, a comprehensive investigation of the heterogeneous charge distribution within the CDs bridges and their tunable band gap demonstrated their significant influence on polarization relaxation and conduction loss. In short, the study presents a novel functionalization theory for CNTs that exhibit remarkable microwave-absorption performance in X-band.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the advent of the 5G era and the continuous advancement of positioning and tracking technologies for military equipment, the issues arising from the presence of electromagnetic (EM) waves have become increasingly severe. As a result, the utilization of absorbing materials has become an essential factor in both daily life and military defense strategies [1–3].

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have attracted extensive attention in microwave absorption applications due to their distinctive sp2 plus π-bond electronic configuration [4,5]. In more recent years, the high-quality EM shielding of CNTs has been successively developed by complexing other electromagnetic materials to tune the dielectric properties. Du et al. [5] proposed the rational decoration of CNTs on Ni/C foams, which exhibited tunable electromagnetic properties and microwave absorption capability. Similarly, Fu et al. [6] reported on CNT composites that integrate polarization and magnetization by coating magnetic nanoparticles onto the surfaces of CNTs. However, the aforementioned non-covalent bonding physical blending methods may result in uneven dispersion of the composites, leading to particle agglomeration, sedimentation, and ultimately, a negative impact on the microwave absorption performance of the composite material. In contrast, the use of covalent bonding methods can not only significantly improve the uniformity of composites but also enhance the electron transfer rate of the system, thereby strengthening the dielectric loss rate. Liu et al. [7] utilized covalent bonding to connect graphene oxide and Fe3O4, achieving a balance between dielectric and magnetic properties while improving impedance matching. The covalent bond acted as a channel for charge transfer, enhancing the dielectric loss capacity and exhibiting excellent EM wave absorption performance. However, a crucial issue is that carbon materials often require pre-treatment to functionalize their surface before they can be covalently bonded with other materials [8,9]. CNTs are often subjected to acid treatment to generate oxygen-containing functional groups with electron-withdrawing properties on their surface [10,11]. While the presence of these oxygen-containing functional groups can cause electron cloud displacement in the π-π-conjugated system due to the inductive effect, ultimately affecting the delocalization of electrons [12]. Specifically, the π-π conjugated sp2 backbones are disrupted, resulting in a decrease in the conductivity of the whole system, which significantly affects the electronic and magnetic transport properties of the system. Hence, a new method that can functionalize CNTs without compromising their electronic transport properties is a highly anticipated solution.

Due to their distinct core-state and surface-state structure, carbon dots (CDs) can act as bridging ligands, facilitating multiple bonding with CNTs through electron cloud overlap. Additionally, CDs possess a small size and high specific area, which make them highly sensitive to doping, functionalization, and defects. CDs with rich surface states can introduce various functional groups when combined with CNTs. Importantly, the core-state structure of CDs is similar to the long-range conjugated π bonds of CNTs, making them highly favorable for electron transport throughout the system. Moreover, CDs offer tunable band gaps and good chemical stability due to the quantum confinement and edge effects [13]. Researchers have demonstrated that electrode materials modified with CDs can effectively address various issues, such as enhancing electronic conductivity, improving ion transmission, and adjusting the size and morphology of metals and metal oxides [14,15]. Yang et al. [16] designed a high-efficiency perovskite solar cell by incorporating an ultrathin layer of CDs between the perovskite and mesoporous titanium dioxide. The CDs acted as a superfast bridge, facilitating electron injection from a halide perovskite into TiO2, resulting in a significant enhancement in photocurrent and PCE of the corresponding solar cells. Additionally, Dai et al. [17] demonstrated that the introduction of CDs into the TiO2 layer of perovskite solar cells could not only improve the photovoltaic performance but also have a strong positive effect on the light stability of perovskite solar cells. Therefore, CDs have great potential in chemical remediation, electrical conduction, recombination, and ligands.

Herein, by introducing CDs with abundant surface functional groups onto the surface of oxidized CNTs (O-CNTs), the CNTs were successfully functionalized while also significantly enhancing their electrical conductivity, ultimately resulting in an improved electromagnetic (EM) properties of the system. The characteristic functional groups on the surfaces of the CDs form amide bonds with the O-CNTs, repairing the electron transport pathway that was previously blocked by the carboxyl group. This bridging of the two materials with a π-conjugated structure accelerates the electron flow between adjacent CNTs, resulting in enhanced electron transport in CDs-CNTs. In this study, the first investigation of the energy transfer process between the CDs and CNTs systems through time-resolved dynamics was conducted. The proposed mechanism of the electron transport pathway was confirmed, and experiments were designed to investigate the effect of quantum confinement and the heterogeneity of electron density within CDs on dielectric loss. Significant advantages of introducing G-CD bridges have been revealed by our investigations. Specifically, the narrower bandgap of the CDs facilitates electron transfer and reduces the conductivity loss of the whole system. Furthermore, the abundance of local electrons inside the CDs leads to stronger dipole polarization due to electron enrichment effects. In summary, the introduction of CDs as a bridge not only functionalized CNTs but also effectively improved the dielectric parameters of O-CNTs, thereby enhancing the EM wave absorption performance of CNTs. The proposed complex of G-CDs-CNTs absorbers exhibited exceptional absorption performance in the X-band. At a thickness of 1.83 mm, the effective absorption bandwidth reached 5.02 GHz, and the EM wave absorption strength of the sample peaked at 38.6 dB at 12.0 GHz at a thickness of 1.9 mm. This research provides new insights and avenues for the development of CNT-system absorbers.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA, AR), ethanolamine (EMA,AR), muli-walled carbon nanotube (CNT, >90%), graphitized hydroxylated carbon nanotube (C-OH-CNT, >99.9%) and hydroxylated carbon nanotube (OH-CNT, 95%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Methanol (MeOH, AR), nitric acid (HNO3) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were purchased from Tianjin Kemio Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Methylene chloride (AR) was purchased from Beijing Tongguang Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Silica powder, which specific surface area was 100–200 or 300–400 m2vg−1, was purchased from Guangzhou Keyuan Instrument and Equipment Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ m) was utilized in the preparation and the analysis of CNT samples.

A specific amount of CNTs underwent acidification with concentrated H2SO4 and concentrated HNO3 (at a ratio of 3:1) and were heated to 80 °C for 2 h in a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor. Following cooling to room temperature, the acidified CNTs were thoroughly washed with ultrapure water until reaching neutral pH. The resulting CNTs were then dispersed in ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the crude products of N-free-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: N-free-CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal synthesis method, using CA as the precursor. Specifically, 3.22 mmol of CA was added to 30 mL of ultrapure water and vigorously stirred until a clear solution was achieved. The resulting solution was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 15 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the crude products of B-CDs and G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: B-CDs and G-CDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal synthesis method, where CA and EMA acted as precursors. Specifically, a mixture containing 59.4 mmol of EMA and 3.22 mmol of CA in 30 mL of ultrapure water was vigorously stirred until a clear solution was obtained. The resulting solution was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 160 °C for 8 h and 180 °C for 15 h, respectively. Following these steps, crude products of B-CDs and G-CDs were obtained.

Following the hydrothermal synthesis, the solutions of N-free-CDs, B-CDs and G-CDs underwent purification via silica-gel column chromatography utilizing a 1:8 mixture of methanol and methylene chloride for gradient elution. Subsequently, the solvent was removed from these solutions through rotary evaporation, and they were subsequently redispersed in ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-free-CDs-CNTs, B-CDs-CNTs and G-CDs-CNTs
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the O-CNTs composites, 0.05 g of O-CNTs was added to 30 mL of N-free-CDs (0.08 mg/mL), B-CDs (0.08 mg/mL), and G-CDs (0.08 mg/mL) solutions separately. The mixture was sonicated for 120 min to obtain a homogenous mixture, which was subsequently transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 16 h. Upon completion, the solution was removed and centrifuged, with additional washing to remove any remaining unreacted carbon dots. Finally, the samples were dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C and stored for further use. (The specific synthesis process can be found in Fig. S9.).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs of CDs-CNTs, B-CDs and G-CDs were obtained using an FEI Tecnai G2 S-Twin transmission electron microscope equipped with a field emission gun that operated at 200 kV. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra of CNTs, O-CNTs and CDs-CNTs were obtained using an IRTracer-100 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) by adopting the KBr-pellet technique. the Raman spectras of CNTs, O-CNTs and CDs-CNTs were obtained using a Xplra plus raman (Hrobia). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of CNTs, O-CNTs and CDs-CNTs was performed using a VG ESCALAB MK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer that used an Mg-Kα (1200 eV) excitation source. A photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FLS 980) were used to measure the PL of B-CDs and G-CDs at room temperature using a quartz cell cuvette (10 mm × 10 mm). The TRPL decay curves of CDs and CDs-CNTs were measured using an Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrometer.

All calculations were done used the Dmol3 package based on density function theory [18]. In this study, the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerh (PBE) generalized function for the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) was chosen. Selection of DFT semi-core pseudopotential (DSSP) for relativistic effects, where all structures are spin-unrestricted. During geometry optimization, all atoms were allowed to relax. The convergence criteria of structural relaxation were as follows: 1.0 × 10−5 Hartree on the energy, 2.0 × 10−4 Hartree/nm on the force, 5.0 × 10−4 nm on the displacement, and 1.0 × 10−6 Hartree on the SCF, respectively.

To measure the electromagnetic properties, 7.5 wt% of the sample powder was thoroughly mixed with paraffin and subsequently pressed into coaxial rings with toroidal shapes that featured an outer diameter of 7 mm and an inner diameter of 3.08 mm. The frequency-dependent complex permittivity and permeability were then measured using a vector network analyzer (VAN, Ceyear 3672c) through the T/R coaxial line method in the frequency range of * 2–18 GHz.

Based on the results of the structural characterizations of the CDs-CNTs, it was observed that the introduction of CDs not only functionalized the surface of the CNTs but also enhanced their electronic transport by forming amide bonds with the O-CNTs. The morphology and elemental analysis of the CDs-CNTs were characterized by TEM and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Fig. 1 a). It is evident that nitrogen-containing precursors were added only during the synthesis process of CDs, as shown in Fig. S1. Subsequently, the presence of nitrogen in the composites was confirmed by the EDS elemental mapping. Furthermore, a notable discovery was the observation of a spherical distribution of nitrogen on the surface of the CNTs, in contrast to the uniform distribution of carbon and oxygen on the CNTs (as indicated by the white dashed boxes in Fig. 1b). This distribution morphology is clearly consistent with the morphology for CDs. Fig. S2 depicts the CNTs interwined with each other, with uniform dispersion. As shown in the schematic diagram in Fig. 1c, the CDs are attached to the surface of the CNTs. By utilizing HR-TEM, clear lattice fringes were highlighted in Fig. 1b, with two interplanar spacings of 0.34 nm and 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (002) and (001) lattice planes of graphitic carbon, respectively. Due to the morphological characteristics of CNTs, the (002) lattice planes for graphitic carbon were usually observed under HR-TEM. In addition, the (001) crystal plane, which is the most easily observed plane of CDs, was also found on the surface of CNTs (marked with a yellow dashed boxes). [19–21] More importantly, the regions where these (001) crystal planes exist are also related to the morphology of CDs. The lattice was further confirmed in the Fourier-filtered lattice images (Fig. 1d,e). Thus, the analysis of the morphology confirms the attachment of CDs onto the surface of the CNTs.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometry (FTIR) spectra were measured to analyze the surface functional groups of CNTs, O-CNTs and CDs-CNTs. As shown in Fig. 1f, a series of typical peaks of CNTs, such as the CC (1620 cm−1) and C-C (1201 cm−1) bond groups, with stretching vibrations in the regions appeared in all the modified CNT samples. The surface of CNTs was lightly oxidized under moist air for a long time, which led to the observation of stretching vibrations of O-H, C-O, and C-H [22]. Moreover, the distinct absorption band located at 1656 cm−1 was attributed to the vibration of CO for O-CNTs, indicating the successful functionalization of carboxyl groups on CNTs. The introduction of carboxyl groups during oxidation led to significant changes in the surface, which could improve the bonding ability of CNTs. Correspondingly, the delocalization properties of electrons within the sp2 domains were partially disrupted. In addition to the observation of absorption bands corresponding to conventional functional groups, a notable enhencement of an absorption band at 1656 cm−1 and the emergence of a new N-H vibration at 1607 cm−1 were detected in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-CNTs. To better illustrate the changes in absorption peaks before and after the composite, the FT-IR spectrum data of the three samples were compared and contrasted in Fig. S3, using the same reference baseline. These distinct vibrations were identified as the functional groups of the amide bond (Fig. 1f; pink illustration) [23], providing evidence for the successful bonding between CNTs and CDs. Furthermore, the introduction of CDs with abundant surface functional groups (such as amino and hydroxyl groups) successfully functionalized CNTs [24]. The graphitic-carbon content of CNTs, O-CNTs, and CDs-CNTs was verified by calculating the ratio of the intensity of the D peak to that of the G peak (ID/IG) in the Raman spectra of each material, as shown in Fig. 1g. Due to the crystallization and growth process, CDs generate numerous active functional groups on their surface and many defects in their carbon core, resulting in lower crystallinity [25]. Consequently, the bonding of CNTs with CDs leads to a increase in the ID/IG ratio from 0.625 to 0.833, indicating a decrease in the degree of graphitization. Furthermore, the EIS spectrum in Fig. 1h demonstrates a significant reduction in the electronic transport capability of the O-CNTs, while the electronic transport capability of the O-CNTs is restored upon bonding with CDs, indicating a recovery of the delocalization properties of the π electrons that were previously disrupted by oxidation. Moreover, XPS analysis was conducted to investigate the relative elemental content and surface structure of the CDs-CNTs. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the binding energy peaks for the variations in the proportion of major elements (C, N, and O) among the three sample groups were consistent with the previously described structural features. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s are presented in Fig. 1i-k. The C 1s spectrum of the three sample groups exhibited four peaks at 284.1, 284.8, 286.1, and 290.1 eV, which were attributed to CC, C–C, CO, and –COOH, respectively [26]. Simultaneously, the concentration of surface functional groups underwent corresponding changes due to both the oxidation of CNTs and the attachment of CDs. The O 1s spectra (Fig. 1j) displayed two peaks at 531.1 and 532.5 eV, which were attributed as C–O and CO, respectively [19]. The oxidation of CNTs resulted in an increase in the CO content from 0.54% to 2.85%, and the subsequent introduction of oxygen-rich CDs onto the surface of CNTs through the formation of amide bonds caused an additional increase in the CO content (from 2.85% to 3.85%). It is worth noting that among all the samples, only CDs-CNTs exhibited a binding energy peak in the N1s spectrum. The observed peaks at 399.1, 400.1, and 401.1 eV in the N1s spectra of CDs-CNTs were assigned to the C–N bond, graphitic N bond, and N–H bond, respectively. This provides direct evidence of the successful bonding between CDs and CNTs through the formation of amide bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The electron-transfer kinetic process of CDs-CNTs composites
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the action of an external electromagnetic field, carriers within dielectric materials move directionally to generate induced current that converts electromagnetic waves to heat by the Joule effect, thereby consuming the energy of the electromagnetic waves within the material. The relationship between the intrinsic conductivity and the conduction loss of these materials is given by the following equation, where ε′′ represents the complex permittivity corresponding to the conduction loss of the material, ε0 is the permittivity of free space, σ denotes the conductivity, and ƒ represents the frequency:
(1)
ε ″
=
σ
2 π
ε 0
×
1 f

Based on the theory of free electrons, materials with higher conductivity have a greater ability to generate macroscopic and eddy currents within them. Consequently, these materials exhibit greater losses when exposed to EM waves. However, at high frequencies of oscillation and with further increases in conductivity, the skin effect manifests itself on the surface of these materials. This phenomenon leads to a direct reflection of incident EM waves and an inability to effectively dissipate energy within the material [27]. Compared to pure conductive materials, nanoscale CDs possess an tunable bandgap structure due to their quantum confinement effect. Under specific conditions, thermal vibration can provide enough energy for some electrons below the Fermi level in CDs to break away from the bond of the valence band and become excited to the conduction band, generating carriers and inducing static current. This tunable conductivity property is ideal for meeting the requirements of controlling the conductive loss of absorbers.

In previous structural characterization, the bonding of CDs to O-CNTs through an amide bond has been successfully demonstrated. To delve deeper into the interactions between CDs and O-CNTs, the optical properties of CDs before and after assembly were further investigate. Fig. 2 a shows the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of both CDs and CNTs in an aqueous solution are presented. It is noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity exhibited a significant reduction upon the bonding of CDs and CNTs. This observation suggests that the carriers generated from the excited state undergone energy dissipation through pathways other than photon emission in the CDs-CNTs complex [28]. To gain deeper understanding of the dynamics of exciton recombination, the PL decay curves of the CDs and CDs-CNTs were collected (Fig. 2b). These decay curves were fitted using the exponential function specified in Eqs. (2) and (3), where I(t) is the luminescence intensities, A1 and A2 are constants and represent the relative amplitudes of each decay component, t is time, τave is the average lifetime, and τ1, and τ2 are fitting parameters related to the decay rates of the luminescent centers. The fitting analysis results of the PL decay curves are shown in Table 1 . The kinetic curves demonstrate that the decay of CDs prior to their combination with CNTs follows a single-exponential decay process, characterized by a relatively slow decay rate.
(2)
I
( t )
=
A 1
exp
( -
t
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
( -
t
τ 2
)
+ . . .
(3)
τ
ave
=
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 1
τ 1 2
+ . . .
A 2
τ 2
+
A 2
τ 2
+ . . .

Yet, the fluorescence decay behavior underwent a significant alteration upon the bonding of CDs with CNTs. The observed data points no longer fit a single-exponential decay model, but instead require a double-exponential decay function. Additionally, the newly emerged decay channel not only occupies a larger portion of the fluorescence decay, but also significantly accelerates the PL decay rate. Based on these observations, we have concluded that when CDs are bonded with CNTs, the majority of the excited-state electrons undergo rapid non-radiative transitions and fall into deep electron traps, which precludes their return to the ground state via photon emission [28]. The observed weak PL emission in CDs-CNTs is in line with this finding. Therefore, it can be concluded that the electron transfer between CDs and CNTs primarily occurs through non-radiative transition channels [29]. Moreover, the spherical structure of CDs facilitates electron transfer throughout the entire system of CNTs by forming amide bonds through the bonds at each angle. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs was illustrated in Fig. 2c, which depicts the energy transfer process. Prior to the bonding of CDs with CNTs, the recombination of hole and electron in the form of an excitons leads to the emission of a photon. Upon bonding, the energy of the excited state is transferred to the CNTs system through non-radiative transition pathways via the amide bond, leading to the weakening of the PL emission peak. The electronic and geometric properties of a system are critical factors influencing its conductivity. In a solvothermal environment, a de hydrolysis process occurs between ethanolamine and citric acid, resulting in the formation of nitrogen-rich CDs with sp2 C-N hybridization in the cores and abundant amino groups on the surface [25,30]. In order to infer the reasons for the non-radiative processes involving the amide bond in the system of CDs-CNTs, a carbon dot layer consisting of 24 carbon atoms and 2 amino groups was constructed and optimized to compare the differential charge density distribution of CDs and CNTs under covalent bonding (amide bond) and van der Waals interactions (Fig. 2de). Examining the transfer and accumulation of electrons along the y and z axes in CDs-CNTs system (Fig. 2f), it is evident that the amide bond significantly enhances the electron transfer between the CDs and CNTs. To investigate the phenomenon of electron loss in functionalized CNTs, a (–COOH) fragment was added to the edge of the CNTs to calculate the charge density difference, as shown in Fig. S5. The carboxyl groups, being a typical electron-withdrawing group, induce an inductive effect that causes the displacement of the electron cloud in the π-π-conjugated system, which ultimately affects the electronic transport within the CNTs [12]. Consequently, the CDs form covalent bonds with the CNTs, repairing the electron transport pathway that was blocked by the carboxyl group, and bridging it with a π-π-conjugated structure. This accelerates the flow of electrons between adjacent CNTs and enhances the electron transport in CDs-CNTs.

The bridging effect of CDs plays a crucial role in enhancing microwave absorption. Hence, conducting further studies on the physicochemical properties of CDs to improve polarization relaxation and conduction loss would be an efficient strategy. Two sets of CDs, named B-CDs and G-CDs, were prepared by controlling the degree of polymerization and carbonization to investigate their effect on the energy levels [31]. TEM showed that the average size of B-CDs and G-CDs was 1.86 and 2.68 nm, respectively (Fig. 3 a, b). The larger size of G-CDs was attributed to the increase in the sp2 domains due to the high degree of carbonization. Based on the quantum confinement effect, different molecular orbitals could be constructed in CDs using different sizes of quantum dots through energy-level gradient engineering [32,33]. The augmentation of the CC content has resulted in a heightened degree of delocalization of the π-π-electron system, thereby leading to a reduction in the energy gap and consequent red-shift of the emission wavelength of CDs. This notion is further corroborated by the PL spectra (Fig. 3c), which show that the emission peak of G-CDs has shifted by 35 nm from 426 to 461 nm, highlighting a narrower optical band gap exhibited by G-CDs. An inset of Fig. 3c presents a plausible mechanism for the two CDs. To gain a deeper understanding of the anisotropic PL exhibited by CDs, first-principles calculations founded on density functional theory (DFT) were conducted. For simplicity, the models are monolayer six-membered-rings consisting of graphitic C and graphitic N plates and without Surface functional groups added (the small size model represents B-CDs and the large size model represents G-CDs). In the computational results, an energy gap of approximately 0.0142 and 0.049 eV (Fig. 3e) between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of two models was observed. It is widely accepted that a smaller bandgap means an increased ability to excite charge carriers, thereby enhancing electrical conductivity [34,35]. Additionally, thermodynamic research demonstrates that the heterogeneity of the material plays a significant role in the space charge polarization [36]. In previous research, nitrogen-doped CDs were found to be effective in regulating the structure of carbon cores. This is owing to the physical properties and particle size of nitrogen atoms, which enable them to partially replace graphitic C in said cores. To investigate the local inhomogeneities of the electron distribution, the electrostatic potential map of both nitrogen-doped CDs and non-nitrogen-doped CDs (referred to as N-free-CDs) was calculated, as depicted in Fig. 3f. Notably, as graphitic N forms within the CDs, an enrichment effect is observed in the electron region surrounding the N atom. This observation suggests that the polarization of the CDs increases significantly. However, since the dissimilar chemical properties of the two elements, the introduction of N atoms leads to a shift of the electron cloud that in turn changes the distribution density of electrons. It can be speculated that the abundance of local electrons in the CDs due to electron enrichment effects, leads to stronger dipole polarization for enhancing microwave loss.

The EM wave absorption performance of a dielectric material depends greatly on its complex permittivity (
ε =
ε ′
+ i
ε ″
), where ε′ reprensents the ability of the absorbing materials to store the electrical energy, and ε″ reflects its ability to dissipate the electrical energy [35,37,38]. Due to the small values, the magnetic permeability (
μ =
μ ′
+ i
μ ″
) is not within our range of consistency. Fig. 4 a shows the fluctuations of ε′ and ε″ values with respect to the frequency of the incident electromagnetic waves for various materials, including untreated CNTs, oxidized CNTs (O-CNTs), composites of CNTs without N doping of CDs (N-free-CDs-CNTs), composites of CNTs and B-CDs (B-CDs-CNTs), and composites of CNTs and G-CDs (G-CDs-CNTs). Evidently, the complex permittivity values of O-CNTs are relatively small, indicating that the oxidation of carbon nanotubes, while introducing functional groups, has a significant effect on their EM wave absorption performance. Among the various absorbing systems, G-CDs-CNTs exhibit the highest dielectric loss capacity. The dielectric loss parameters (ε′′/ε′) and attenuation constant (α) confirm this (shown in Fig. S6). A higher complex dielectric constant of G-CDs-CNTs indicates that the attachment of CDs can significantly restore the complex dielectric constant of O-CNTs. Furthermore, the high dielectric loss value demonstrates that the attachment of CDs not only introduces functional groups, but also enhances the electromagnetic parameter characteristics of CNTs, making them more conducive to microwave absorption. The ε′ values of the three types of CNTs attached with CDs (N-free-CDs, B-CDs-CNTs, G-CDs-CNTs) show little difference, indicating that the amide bonds formed between the CDs and the CNTs repair the electron transport pathway that was blocked by the carboxyl group. Through this bonding, the CDs bridge adjacent CNTs, accelerating electron flow between them, and enhancing electron transport in the CDs-CNTs. In addition, although the group of N-free-CDs restores the conductivity for CNTs, the introduction of CDs without nitrogen doping does not increase the dielectric loss value (ε′′) on CNTs, consistent with our previous calculations. The abundance of local electrons in CDs, resulting from the electron enrichment effects caused by nitrogen atom doping, leads to stronger dipole polarization, resulting in higher dielectric loss values. The reflection loss (RL) is a crucial parameter that directly reflects the electromagnetic wave absorption performance of a material, as expressed by the following equation:
(4)
RL
(dB) = 20lg
Z
in
-
Z 0
Z
in
+
Z 0
(5)
Z
in
=
Z 0
μ r
ε r
tanh
( j
2 π f d
c
μ r
ε r
)
Where Zin is the input impedance, c is the speed of light in a vacuum, d is the thickness of the wave-absorbing material, and h is the Planck constant. Significantly, these results were consistent with the trends predicted from our simulations. For G-CDs-CNTs, the range of RL < −10 dB is the widest, indicating the optimal electromagnetic wave absorption performance. The effective absorption bandwidth at a thickness of 1.83 mm reaches 5.02 GHz (10.77–15.79 GHz) and the electromagnetic wave absorption strength of the sample peaks is 38.6 dB at 12.0 GHz and has a thickness of 1.9 mm (Fig. 4). To evaluate the impact of other functionalizations on the EM wave absorption performance of CNTs, the complex permittivity and magnetic permeability of C-OH-CNTs and OH-CNTs were tested as supplementary information. The results revealed that functionalization considerably diminished the absorption performance of CNTs, similar to that of O-CNTs (Fig. S7 and Fig. S8). This study demonstrates that the functionalizing CNTs while not affecting their electromagnetic parameters has significant implications for the widespread application of CNTs in the field of electromagnetic wave absorption. Through experimental characterization and theoretical calculation, we have proposed a plausible microwave absorption mechanism for G-CDs-CNT based on specific binding sites of the amide bond (as shown in Fig. 5 ). The characteristic functional groups on the surfaces of the CDs form amide bonds between CNTs, bridging them with π-conjugated structures and repairing the blocked electron transport pathway triggered by carboxyl groups. Moreover, the region around N atoms in carbon cores exhibits enrichment effects leading to heterogeneous inside charge distribution, resulting in significant polarization of CDs. As a result, the incident electromagnetic wave energy is consumed through conduction loss and dipolar polarization.

In this study, we aimed to enhance the bonding ability and electromagnetic properties of CNTs by introducing CDs onto their surface. Comprehensive chemical and morphological characterizations were carried out to determine the nature of the chemical bonding and structural changes resulting from the interaction between CDs and CNTs. It was confirmed that the surface-state functional groups of CDs not only functionalize CNTs but also form amide bonds that bridge the sp2 domains in CDs and CNTs, thereby accelerating electron flow between adjacent CNTs by repairing the electron transport pathway blocked by carboxyl groups. Time-resolved dynamics studies were also performed to investigate the energy transfer process for the CDs-CNTs system, supporting the proposed mechanism of the electron transport pathway. First-principles calculations and experimental designs were employed to demonstrate that modulation of the quantum confinement effect and heterogeneity of electron density inside CDs can effectively improve the electromagnetic parameters of the composite. The smaller bandgap of CDs indicates an improved capability for exciting charge carriers and enhancing the electrical conductivity of the CDs-CNTs system. Furthermore, the abundance of local electrons inside CDs leads to stronger dipole polarization due to electron enrichment effects. The CDs-CNTs composite in this study exhibited remarkable broadband microwave absorption in the X-band. With a thickness of 1.83 mm, the effective absorption bandwidth reached 5.02 GHz. and the EM wave absorption strength of the sample peaked at 38.6 dB at 12.0 GHz at a thickness of 1.9 mm. This research provides new insights and avenues for the development of CNT-system absorbers.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi (No.2022JQ-356); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022M723884); Special Support Program for High-level Talents of Shaanxi Province (No. 2020-44). We also thank The Youth Innovation Team of Shaanxi Universities for the guidance on methodology.
